Occupational health and safety issues in human-robot collaboration: State of the art and open challenges
========================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Antonio Giallanza a, Giada La Scalia b, Rosa Micale a, Concetta Manuela La Fata b

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   ‚ÄĘDesigning and implementing safe and ergonomic solutions. 
*   ‚ÄĘCurrent trends of HRC-related articles by topics and application areas. 
*   ‚ÄĘOverview on Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) concerns in collaborative environments. 
*   ‚ÄĘFuture lines of research to transfer collaborative robotics from the lab scale to the shop floor. 

Abstract
--------

Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC) refers to the interaction of workers and robots in a shared workspace. Owing to the integration of the industrial automation strengths with the inimitable cognitive capabilities of humans, HRC is paramount to move towards advanced and sustainable production systems. Although the overall safety of collaborative robotics has increased over time, further research efforts are needed to allow humans to operate alongside robots, with awareness and trust. Numerous safety concerns are open, and either new or enhanced technical, procedural and organizational measures have to be investigated to design and implement inherently safe and ergonomic automation solutions, aligning the systems performance and the human safety. Therefore, a bibliometric analysis and a literature review are carried out in the present paper to provide a comprehensive overview of Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) issues in HRC. As a result, the most researched topics and application areas, and the possible future lines of research are identified. Reviewed articles stress the central role played by humans during collaboration, underlining the need to integrate the human factor in the hazard analysis and risk assessment. Human-centered design and cognitive engineering principles also require further investigations to increase the worker acceptance and trust during collaboration. Deepened studies are compulsory in the healthcare sector, to investigate the social and ethical implications of HRC. Whatever the application context is, the implementation of more and more advanced technologies is fundamental to overcome the current HRC safety concerns, designing low-risk HRC systems while ensuring the system productivity.

## AMR Safety Strategies for Human-Robot Collaboration on the Floor

Ensuring safety in human-robot collaboration involves implementing key AMR strategies such as:

- Designing clear pedestrian zones
- Implementing sensor-based collision avoidance
- Conducting regular safety audits

These strategies ensure seamless and secure operations.

May 9, 2025

In the rapidly evolving landscape ‚Äčof‚Ā£ modern ‚Ā£manufacturing ‚ÄĆand logistics, the‚Ā£ integration of Autonomous Mobile‚Äć Robots (AMRs) into the workplace has transformed operational dynamics, bringing about unprecedented efficiency‚ÄĆ and flexibility. However, with this advancement ‚ĀĘcomes the critical responsibility of ensuring that human-robot collaboration‚ÄĆ is not only efficient but‚ÄĆ also ‚ĀĘsafe. AMR safety strategies on the shop ‚ĀĘfloor are paramount to ‚Äćminimizing risks and ‚Äčenhancing‚Äć productivity. ‚ÄĆThis article delves into the ‚ĀĘcore‚Ā§ strategies for ensuring safe interaction between humans and AMRs, offering ‚Äčinsights into the implementation of proactive safety measures.

AMRs are equipped with advanced sensors and algorithms designed for navigation and‚ĀĘ task execution in dynamic environments. However, achieving seamless human-robot collaboration requires thorough strategies:

Through this exploration, ‚Ā§we aim to provide a ‚Äćcomprehensive understanding of the best practices and technologies that safeguard‚Ā§ interactions ‚ÄĆon the floor, ultimately‚ÄĆ fostering a collaborative environment that capitalizes on ‚Ā§the capabilities of AMRs‚Ā£ while [prioritizing‚Äč human safety](https://www.innorobix.com/what-safety-features-do-amrs-have/ "What safety features do AMRs have").

### Ensuring Safe Interaction: ‚ÄćKey Safety Features Integrated into ‚Ā£amrs for Human Proximity

Autonomous‚Ā§ mobile ‚Ā£Robots (AMRs) are equipped with a robust array of **safety features** ‚Äć designed to ensure seamless and safe interaction with human ‚Äćworkers. One of the‚ÄĆ essential ‚Ā£technologies‚Ā£ utilized is **LIDAR (light Detection and Ranging)**, which empowers‚Äč AMRs with real-time 360-degree object detection and environmental mapping. ‚ÄćThis technology‚Äč allows AMRs to dynamically adjust their‚Äć paths in ‚Ā£real-time, ensuring they can [safely navigate complex environments](https://www.innorobix.com/using-amrs-to-support-lights-out-manufacturing-shifts/ "Using AMRs to Support Lights-Out Manufacturing Shifts")‚Äč like ‚Ā£[busy factory floors](https://www.innorobix.com/using-amrs-for-just-in-time-delivery-in-automotive-production/ "Using AMRs for Just-in-Time Delivery in Automotive Production"). As ‚Ā£an example, in a warehouse‚Ā§ where personnel frequently ‚Äčcross paths‚ĀĘ with mobile‚Äć robots,‚ÄĆ an AMR equipped with LIDAR can slow ‚Ā£down or stop entirely, avoiding collisions and maintaining an accident-free‚ÄĆ workspace.‚Ā§ Additionally, advanced **safety-rated sensors** ‚Ā§ are integrated to‚Ā£ discern between objects and‚ÄĆ human presence, enabling safer operations even‚Ā£ in ‚Ā£densely populated areas.

In‚ĀĘ addition to hardware features, ‚Äčamrs leverage refined‚Äć **software algorithms** and **predictive analytics**. Advanced software ‚Äćalgorithms are tasked with interpreting sensor data to ensure proactive responses in ‚Äčhuman-robot interactions. Predictive analytics enables AMRs to anticipate potential safety hazards ‚Ā£by learning from past interactions and optimizing response protocols. Notable ‚Äčindustry players‚Ā§ like OTTO‚ÄĆ motors‚Äć and MiR have‚ÄĆ developed **intuitive ‚Ā§user interfaces** ‚ĀĘenabling facility‚Äć managers to‚Ā£ customize ‚ĀĘsafety zones and interaction‚Ā§ limits directly within ‚ĀĘthe ‚Ā§robot‚Äôs software. This feature ‚Äčaids in delineating safe operating‚Äć spaces and‚Ā£ configuring alert settings as‚Ā§ per real-time floor conditions. Moreover, when integrated with a‚Ā£ facility‚Äôs **Workplace‚Ā§ Management Systems ‚ĀĘ(WMS) ‚ĀĘor Enterprise ‚Ā§Resource Planning (ERP)** systems, these AMRs can align their ‚Äčtasks and routes seamlessly in‚Ā§ coordination with‚Ā§ human‚Äč operators,‚ÄĆ safeguarding not only‚Äč efficiency but optimizing safety protocols.

### Best ‚ĀĘPractices ‚Ā£for‚Ā§ Designing Collaborative ‚ĀĘSpaces: Minimizing‚Äč Risks‚ÄĆ between Humans‚Ā£ and Robots

To design collaborative‚Äč spaces that minimize‚ÄĆ risks between humans and robots, one must first ensure ‚Ā§that **clear zones** are ‚Äčdelineated using both ‚Äćphysical‚Ā§ and software-based barriers.Physical barriers can include floor markings or low-profile fencing that define robot-only lanes, while advanced sensing technologies ‚ÄĆcan be employed to establish‚Ā§ virtual boundaries. For instance, ‚ÄĆthe implementation of‚ÄĆ light curtains or LiDAR sensors ensures ‚Äćthat AMRs ‚Ā§automatically halt when a‚Äč human breaches their operational ‚ĀĘspace, effectively reducing collision risks. **Adaptive traffic‚Äč management systems**, similar to those used by OTTO, dynamically alter AMR paths based on real-time human activity, further enhancing safety.

Another critical practice ‚ĀĘis **establishing robust‚Äć communication protocols** between‚Äć human operators and AMRs.Visual indicators, such‚ÄĆ as LED strips or ‚Ā£screens ‚Ā§displaying ‚ĀĘrobot intentions, coupled with audible‚ĀĘ alerts, provide crucial facts to nearby workers. Additionally,‚Ā§ training employees in **robot awareness** fosters a culture‚Äć of‚Ā£ safety, as ‚Äčseen at facilities using MiR‚Äôs AMRs,‚Ā£ where workers receive comprehensive‚ÄĆ onboarding‚Äč sessions. Employers can ‚Ā£encourage regular feedback through digital platforms to continuously fine-tune AMR operations and human interactions, thereby ensuring a symbiotic workspace.‚Äč Adopting these best practices ‚ĀĘnot only minimizes risks ‚Äćbut also ensures the seamless integration‚ÄĆ of AMRs into human-centric environments.

### Implementing Real-Time Monitoring ‚Ā§Systems: Enhancing Safety through continuous Feedback

To bolster safety in ‚Äčenvironments where humans and AMRs work side-by-side, [implementing [real-time monitoring](https://www.innorobix.com/mes-integration-for-cnc-shops-connecting-multiple-machine-brands-to-ignition/ "MES Integration for CNC Shops: Connecting Multiple Machine Brands to Ignition") systems](https://www.innorobix.com/preventing-overloads-in-conveyor-driven-recovery-lines/ "Preventing Overloads in Conveyor-Driven Recovery Lines") ‚Ā§can serve as ‚Äča cornerstone strategy.These systems facilitate continuous feedback by utilizing a network‚ĀĘ of‚ÄĆ sensors, cameras, and ‚Äćsoftware analytics to‚Äč scrutinize the robot‚Äôs activity and‚Äć the surrounding ‚Äčenvironment. For instance, a manufacturing plant operating with MiR‚ĀĘ robots might install LiDAR sensors to create dynamic maps that detect obstacles or ‚Äćhuman presence in ‚Ā£real-time. These maps allow the AMR to adjust its trajectory‚ĀĘ to‚Äć avoid potential collisions, ensuring a safer workspace. Additionally, integrating‚ÄĆ these monitoring systems with machine ‚ĀĘlearning algorithms can predict hazardous situations before they occur by analyzing patterns and scenarios‚Ā§ that ‚Ā§frequently lead to accidents.

Companies such as‚Äč OTTO‚Äč Motors have demonstrated how effective real-time monitoring systems can be in promoting safety during human-robot collaboration. By ‚ĀĘcontinuously capturing data points through ‚Äčvarious‚Ā§ interfaces,‚Ā£ these systems can ‚Ā£[trigger automatic alerts](https://www.innorobix.com/managing-tool-calibration-data-with-ignition-and-plc-interfaces/ "Managing Tool Calibration Data with Ignition and PLC Interfaces") or shutdowns if predetermined safety thresholds are exceeded. This proactive approach not only protects personnel but also‚ÄĆ enhances operational efficiency.Key features of such systems often include:

Ultimately,these measures reduce‚Äć accidents,downtime,and can foster an atmosphere‚Äć of trust‚ÄĆ between ‚Ā§human operators‚Äč and ‚Äčtheir robotic counterparts.

### Training and Development Programs: Preparing Workforce for Effective Human-Robot Collaboration

Promoting ‚Äča triumphant environment‚Ā§ where humans and robots can ‚Ā£coexist on the manufacturing floor requires targeted **training‚ÄĆ and development ‚ĀĘprograms**. These initiatives should focus on imparting knowledge of safety protocols and operational guidelines ‚Ā§concerning AMRs. as ‚Ā£robots rely‚ÄĆ heavily ‚ÄĆon technology ‚Ā§such as sensors, ‚ÄĆcameras, and AI to‚Äč process their surroundings and make autonomous decisions, understanding‚Äć their operational ‚Äćprinciples is crucial.‚Ā§ Training sessions frequently enough involve real-world demonstrations ‚ÄĆof AMR behavioral responses ‚Äčto proximity, reinforcing how‚ĀĘ they interact with human‚ÄĆ presence. Introducing case studies, such as‚ĀĘ the seamless implementation of AMRs at BMW‚Äôs Spartanburg facility, can vividly illustrate effective collaboration where ‚ÄĆrobots adeptly maneuver‚Ā£ while ‚Äčhumans carry out tasks ‚Äćconcurrently.

### Q&A

**Q&A: Evaluating AMR Safety Strategies‚ĀĘ for ‚Ā§Human-Robot Collaboration**

**Q1: What ‚Äćare ‚Äćthe key safety strategies for integrating AMRs into human-populated ‚ĀĘworkspaces?**

**A1:** Implementing‚ĀĘ effective‚Ā§ safety strategies for amrs in environments where humans are present involves:

‚Äć ‚Äď Conduct ‚ÄĆcomprehensive risk assessments to identify ‚ĀĘpotential hazards related to AMR‚Äć operations.  
‚Ā£

‚Äď Equip AMRs with the latest‚Äć safety features such as LiDAR sensors, cameras, and ultrasonic sensors for ‚Äčreal-time obstacle detection.

‚Äď ‚Ā§Establish well-defined‚ÄĆ pathways ‚Äčand‚ÄĆ zones for AMR operations to minimize unexpected crossings‚Ā§ with ‚Ā§human‚Äč workers.

‚Äď Implement‚Ā£ dynamic hazard‚Äć recognition and avoidance technologies to enable AMRs ‚Ā§to respond to unexpected obstacles‚Äć and people.

**example:** A factory leverages ‚ÄčLiDAR-equipped AMRs‚Ā§ to navigate through bustling production lines, employing dynamic path‚Ā§ adjustments when‚Äč workers are detected within predefined proximity.

**Q2: How do collaborative AMRs ensure compliance with safety standards?**

**A2:** Collaborative AMRs comply with safety standards‚Äč through:

‚Äč -‚Äć Manufacturers design AMRs to conform to industry‚Äć standards such as ISO 3691-4,‚ĀĘ ensuring safety requirements are met.

‚Äč- AMRs are integrated with‚Ā£ centralized safety systems that ‚Äčmonitor and control interactions between robots and humans.

‚Äď Use of remotely accessible diagnostic tools for ‚Äčreal-time monitoring ‚ÄĆand safety compliance verification.

**example:** An automotive‚Äč assembly plant integrates AMRs with an overarching safety management ‚Äčsystem that automatically stops robots when unauthorized personnel are detected in restricted zones.

**Q3: What are‚Äč the ‚ĀĘbest practices for AMR ‚Ā§deployment to enhance safety in a‚Äč SCADA environment?**

**A3:** Best practices include:

‚Äď Ensure AMRs‚ĀĘ are interfaced with SCADA ‚Äćsystems for seamless communication and control, [enhancing real-time monitoring](https://www.innorobix.com/how-does-ignition-scada-improve-operational-efficiency/ "How does Ignition SCADA improve operational efficiency") and safety response.

‚ÄĆ ‚Äď ‚ÄĆConduct regular safety protocol training for both‚ĀĘ human operators and technical teams managing AMRs.  
‚Ā£

‚Äď Implement layered safety checks within the SCADA ‚Äćsystem‚Ā£ to monitor ‚ĀĘAMR status, path integrity, and environmental‚Äć conditions.

**Example:** A food processing facility integrates its AMR fleet with SCADA, using‚Äć [real-time alerts](https://www.innorobix.com/optimizing-cnc-monitoring-ignition-mtconnect-insights/ "Optimizing CNC Monitoring: Ignition & MTConnect Insights") ‚ÄĆto react promptly to‚ĀĘ any deviations from standard‚Ā£ operating procedures.

**Q4: How can technology mitigate human-robot interaction risks?**

**A4:** ‚ÄčTechnology solutions that mitigate risks include:

-‚Äć Leverage sophisticated vision systems to recognize and track‚Ā§ human ‚Äćpresence, adjusting AMR paths as necessary.  
‚Äć

-‚ĀĘ Incorporate AI‚Äć algorithms for [real-time decision-making](https://www.innorobix.com/best-practices-for-using-ignition-in-manufacturing-environments/ "Best Practices for Using Ignition in Manufacturing Environments") to enhance situational awareness and anticipate human actions.

‚Äď Utilize proximity-detection systems that ‚Ā§ensure adequate‚Äč separation ‚Äćbetween AMRs and humans.

**Example:** In a ‚ĀĘdistribution center, ‚ÄćAMRs are equipped with AI-driven path‚ÄĆ prediction‚Äć algorithms that‚ÄĆ modify routes to avoid areas with dense human activity.

These strategies, practices, and technologies collectively contribute to creating‚ÄĆ a safer and ‚Ā§more efficient environment for human-robot ‚Äćcollaboration in industrial settings.

ensuring‚Ā§ safety in human-robot collaboration on the manufacturing floor is ‚Äćparamount,and implementing effective safety strategies ‚Äčfor AMRs is crucial for maximizing efficiency without compromising worker well-being.‚ÄĆ Key takeaways include:

enriched_points.expanded_content: Safety is paramount in Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC) to prevent accidents, ensuring a secure working environment where humans and robots can effectively interact without risks.
Establishing comprehensive safety protocols is essential for minimizing hazards. These include risk assessments, safety distances, and emergency stop functions to safeguard human operators.

enriched_points.point_title: Benefits of a safe collaborative environment
enriched_points.expanded_content: A safe collaborative environment enhances worker confidence, increases productivity, and fosters innovation, ultimately leading to improved operational efficiency in automated systems.
